title: Ossoff: Democratic midterm victories would do more than ‘just restore checks and balances’
description: Democratic Sen. Jon Ossoff (Ga.) said Friday his party, if it takes control of both chambers of Congress after the midterms, will “rebuke” President Trump for the remainder of his term. “Our answer wi...
fullText:
Democratic Sen. Jon Ossoff (Ga.) said Friday his party, if it takes control of both chambers of Congress after the midterms, will "rebuke" President Trump for the remainder of his term.

"Our answer will be massive. Our answer will be decisive," Ossoff said at a rally in the Peach State. "We won't just restore checks and balances for the next two years."

"We will rebuke him so decisively that no president will dare try this kind of brazen corruption and abuse again for centuries."

Ossoff, who is running for reelection against Rep. Mike Collins (R-Ga.), has frequently criticized Trump during the latter's second term — particularly over concerns about the president's financial dealings while in office.

During his Friday remarks, the Georgia Democrat also went after Trump for claiming the day prior that China accessed U.S. voter rolls ahead of the 2020 presidential election. Ossoff said the president was reairing his "grievances" about that race, which he lost to former President Biden.

"The same man whose grievances stirred up that violent mob that sacked the Capitol, still cannot let go of his shame and humiliation at having been dealt that defeat by voters here in Georgia and across the country," Ossoff added, referring to the Jan. 6, 2021, attack on the U.S. Capitol.

"Instead of focusing on the nation's needs, as always, he nurses his grievances," continued the first-term senator, who sits on the Senate Intelligence Committee. "As always, he forces the rest of us to help him process his shame. As always, he centers his own fear of the accountability that he will face when we win decisively this fall."

As for the chances that Ossoff's words will prove true and Democrats take back the Senate in November, Decision Desk HQ (DDHQ) gives the minority party a 42 percent chance of doing so. DDHQ also gives Democrats a 61 percent chance of taking back the House.

In order to flip the Senate, Democrats must retain the 47 seats they already hold and flip four seats held by Republicans.

Three GOP seats, those held by Sens. Dan Sullivan (Alaska), Susan Collins (Maine) and Jon Husted (Ohio), are "toss-ups" according to the nonpartisan Cook Political Report, which also rates the race between Democrat Roy Cooper and Republican Michael Whatley to succeed retiring Sen. Thom Tillis (R-N.C.) as a Democratic lean.

Ossoff's reelection bid against Collins is also in the Democratic lean column, according to the Cook Political Report.

title: Trump administration opens investigation into Harvard's financial aid for international students
description: The Justice Department is investigating whether Harvard University is allowing Chinese donors to create scholarships that exclude American students, adding to the barrage of federal inquiries the Trum...
fullText:
The Justice Department is investigating whether Harvard University is allowing Chinese donors to create scholarships that exclude American students, adding to the barrage of federal inquiries the Trump administration has opened in its battle against the Ivy League school.

Justice Department officials announced the new review Monday, saying Harvard's foreign funding disclosures "raised concerns" about possible discrimination against U.S. students. The agency did not specify which scholarship programs are being targeted and emphasized it "has not reached any conclusions."

The investigation is aimed at Harvard's "China-based financial aid programs," the Justice Department said. The agency said Chinese donors have given money to Harvard to support financial aid programs limited to students from certain countries. That restriction could amount to illegal discrimination based on country of origin, the department said.

"Schools cannot take federal dollars and then turn around and accept money from foreign sources to give financial aid that deliberately excludes American citizens — doing so is illegal, and we will stop it wherever we find it," said Harmeet Dhillon, who leads the department's Civil Rights Division.

Harvard said it was reviewing the Justice Department notice.

"Harvard follows the law for required reporting of donations and, consistent with our legal obligations under Title VI, does not unlawfully discriminate on the basis of race, ethnicity, or national origin in allocating financial aid," the university said in a written statement.

In a statement, Liu Chang, a spokesperson for the Chinese embassy in Washington, said China-U.S. education cooperation is mutually beneficial. "China maintains that normal education and academic exchange should not be disrupted for political purposes," Liu said.

Like many U.S. universities, Harvard accepts foreign funding through direct donations or contracts. The Trump administration has pushed for greater transparency around outside money, warning that accepting funding from foreign adversaries is a national security risk.

Federal disclosures from Harvard show it has accepted $630 million from Chinese sources over a period of decades. Overall, it has reported about $4.5 billion in foreign funding. The Justice Department cited those figures but did not single out any donors or programs being reviewed.

The investigation is a compliance review, meaning it's initiated by the federal government rather than responding to an outside complaint.

Unlike the vast majority of U.S. universities, Harvard and a small number of other schools provide need-based scholarships to international students on the same basis as U.S. students. Grants and other aid from the federal government are reserved for American students, but Harvard "has its own job and scholarship money available to foreign students," according to a Harvard financial aid website.

In some cases, foreign donors have given money to be used for international students.

In 2014, Pan Shiyi and Zhang Xin, a Chinese couple that co-founded the real estate company SOHO China, donated $15 million to Harvard to provide financial aid for Chinese students. Their gift drew blowback from critics in China who said it was a betrayal to give money to foreign schools instead of Chinese universities.

The Justice Department's announcement makes the unusual legal argument that steering private funding to students of certain countries amounts to a civil rights violation for U.S. students.

"Harvard appears to be accepting these funds and, in adherence to their restrictions, providing student financial aid to foreign students, presumably Chinese, based on their national origin — to the potential detriment of students of other national origins, including American citizens," the agency said.

The Trump administration has repeatedly opened new federal investigations against Harvard in its battle to win obedience from the nation's oldest university.

Harvard has been a prime target in the administration's push to end "wokeness" and fight alleged antisemitism in American higher education. A federal judge has blocked the White House from cutting billions of dollars in federal research funding from Harvard amid the feud.

Even as the legal battle continues, the government has sought new lines of attack, seeking to limit Harvard's ability to enroll foreign students and U.S. military members, and launching investigations into the school's admissions process.

The Associated Press' education coverage receives financial support from multiple private foundations. AP is solely responsible for all content. Find AP's standards for working with philanthropies, a list of supporters and funded coverage areas at AP.org.

title: Trump intervenes as House GOP revolt puts SAVE America Act at center of spending showdown
description: Trump urges House Republicans to unify behind the budget reconciliation bill, but some fiscal hawks balk at $95 billion in new spending with no payfors.
fullText:
President Donald Trump is throwing his weight behind House GOP leaders' latest gambit to pass the SAVE America Act as some fiscal hawks are skeptical of the bill's increase to federal spending while the national debt balloons to $39.5 trillion.

Speaker Mike Johnson, R-La., is spearheading an effort to pass elements of the election integrity bill through the budget reconciliation process, which would sidestep the need for Democratic support in the Senate in order to get the bill to Trump's desk.

The legislation would also add some $73 billion in national security spending as the war between the U.S. and Iran continues after multiple stalled attempts at peace talks.

Gop's $95B Defense Package Survives Critical Hurdle Amid Fiscal Hawk Revolt

"The fact that there is no payfor and no language defunding Planned Parenthood is enough for me to vote no," Rep. Eric Burlison, R-Mo., told Fox News Digital on Monday. "I am open to a dialogue with the administration on how we can ensure that the SAVE language is solid and what we might be able to do with stopping abortions by mail."

Trump took to Truth Social to personally thank Johnson and House Budget Committee members for their work cobbling together the new reconciliation bill, which would include $95 billion in new government spending through 2036.

"I am asking all House Republicans to VOTE YES this week on the Budget Resolution, which is an important first step towards getting a Budget Bill. House Republicans must UNIFY, and fight for THE SAVE AMERICA ACT!" Trump posted.

Gop Civil War Erupts Over Trump's Latest Agenda Push As Key Republicans Threaten To Sink Megabill

He argued the legislation would "get AS MUCH of THE SAVE AMERICA ACT as possible in a Budget Bill, along with aid to our incredible Farmers, and funding for our Military."

The bill, which was rolled out last week, is known as a budget "framework" setting rules for how much money the relevant congressional committees must spend. The details of exactly where the money will go will be determined in the coming weeks by lawmakers on those committees, before those details are patched back together into a massive piece of legislation.

But the framework does not currently have any spending cuts written into it, which immediately raised alarms for conservatives who are wary of additional government spending without cuts to offset it, also called payfors.

Johnson Races To Break House Gop Blockade In High-stakes Meeting With Trump

Reps. Andy Ogles, R-Tenn., and Tim Burchett, R-Tenn., told Fox News Digital they were both concerned about the lack of payfors before Trump's post but left the door open on whether they would ultimately support the bill.

Burchett predicted the legislation would be sent to the Senate, where SAVE America Act elements would be stripped from the bill before it is returned to the House.

"Then I'll probably vote no," he added afterward. He did not immediately answer a follow-up question about whether he supports the original iteration of the bill.

A source familiar with thinking by the conservative House Freedom Caucus left the door open to the group backing the budget framework, but with the caveat that the full SAVE America Act is included in the final legislation.

"The SAVE America Act is our top priority — the president wants it done. Mandatory voter ID and proof of citizenship through reconciliation would be a game-changer for this country. But any watered-down version that makes SAVE policies optional? There will be issues," the source told Fox News Digital.

A senior House GOP aide said, "We can't spend years warning about our fiscal crisis and then abandon payfors the moment they're politically inconvenient. Americans are tired of the all-hat, no-cattle approach to fiscal responsibility."

But some House Freedom Caucus members are supportive of the chosen path, like Budget Committee member Rep. Marlin Stutzman, R-Ind., who told Fox News Digital, "As a fiscal hawk I understand the apprehension of not having a clear payfor in sight, but as an American, I know it is even more irresponsible to have business as usual elections without the security the SAVE America Act will create."

The SAVE America Act — which would create a federal voter ID standard and mandate proof of citizenship in the voter registration process, among other elements — passed the House but has stalled in the Senate, where at least some Democrats would be needed to advance the bill to final passage.

Democrats have argued the bill is tantamount to voter suppression.

House GOP leaders are now pushing to pass elements of the SAVE America Act in the wider reconciliation bill to sidestep that opposition. Budget reconciliation only requires a simple majority to pass in the Senate, lining it up with the House's own threshold.

But that comes with strict rules on what is permissible to include in a budget reconciliation bill, which has traditionally been left up to the discretion of the Senate parliamentarian — a nonpartisan congressional staffer tasked with making those decisions based on the chamber's existing rules.

Original article

title: Nancy Mace reveals Senate decision as Lindsey Graham's sister redraws the race
description: Darline Graham told Sean Hannity "I'm in" after Trump pushed her to seek the Republican nomination in South Carolina's crowded Senate special election.
fullText:
As the field for the race to replace the late Sen. Lindsey Graham takes shape, one prominent lawmaker is declining to dive into the arena hours after the contest was shaken up.

Rep. Nancy Mace, R-S.C., opted against a bid after she strongly suggested that she'd throw her hat into the arena on Monday in a video statement. Last August, Mace launched a campaign for the Republican gubernatorial nomination in the race to succeed term-limited GOP Gov. Henry McMaster, but she finished a disappointing fifth out of five major candidates in last month's Republican primary.

"I want to be straight with you, I'm not running for the U.S. Senate, not because I'm backing down from a fight, but because the one I'm already in matters right now," Mace said. 

"I'm not chasing another title, I'm chasing justice, justice for me, justice for other women and justice for every little girl who was told to stay silent," she continued. "That's where my focus is today, and that's where it's going to stay."

Fox News Channel's 'Hannity' To Air Exclusive First Interview With Sen Darline Graham On Monday

Her decision followed a move by Sen. Darline Graham, R-S.C., to take up her brother's mantle after she was appointed to finish off the remainder of her late brother's term. She made her decision to officially enter the upcoming special election after a push from President Donald Trump.

Graham, during an interview with Fox News' Sean Hannity, acknowledged the "tremendous amount of pressure" to take over the position and run.

"I feel like I can do it, I feel an inner peace about it," Graham said. "Will it be difficult? Yes."

When asked if she had made a decision on what she would do, she told Hannity she had.

Her addition to the growing roster of hopefuls to replace the late lawmaker will likely shake up the race less than one day before candidates can begin officially filing to run for the nomination.

Graham will come up against other challengers in her bid to claim the GOP nomination. Reps. Ralph Norman, R-S.C., Russell Fry, R-S.C., and businessman Mark Lynch, have all publicly committed to running.

And as the filing deadline fast approaches, both Mace and Norman turned their steel against their colleague, Fry, in what's shaping up to be a messy, Palmetto State brawl.

Mace argued that should Fry win the nomination, and subsequently the general election, he would vacate his "Republican House seat until next May."

Republicans Defy Trump, Jump Into South Carolina Senate Race Despite His Handpicked Successor

"That narrows an already razor-thin majority and puts the Speaker's gavel in play," Mace said. "I will never hand Hakeem Jeffries that gift."

"If you're running for two offices at once, you've already told us who you are," she continued. "That's not about politics anymore — that's about power. You're either with the people you serve or you're not."

Fry announced Monday morning that he would enter the race, hours after Norman tossed in his hat, too. Mace has been making public overtures that she wants to jump in, but has so far not made anything official.

A source in Fry's political orbit told Fox News Digital that the lawmaker has "accomplished more than all the potential candidates combined," having passed three bills in his first four years in Congress.

"This is really about giving voters a choice and not a coronation," they said.

Still, the conservative duo are going for blood against Fry, arguing that his bid for the seat is purely politically motivated and jeopardizes the House's already slim majority heading into the midterm elections later this year.

Under South Carolina law, Fry can appear for multiple offices on the ballot. He was unopposed for his primary election for South Carolina's 7th congressional district, and if he wins the nomination, he'll be on the ballot twice for both.

Trump Reveals Who He's Eyeing To Replace Lindsey Graham

Ultimately, he'll have to choose if he wins both, which would trigger another special election to fill his place.

The top two Republicans in the South Carolina Statehouse, South Carolina Senate President Thomas Alexander and South Carolina House Speaker Murrell Smith, said in a statement that the upcoming special election has "highlighted issues in South Carolina law," and noted that, "In the coming weeks, we intend to address these important issues so that we can provide the process available for our citizens."

That is not a problem for either Mace nor Norman, who both ran and failed to clinch the Republican gubernatorial nomination last month.

"SC Republican voters soundly rejected Nancy and Ralph as recently as last month because conservatives are tired of career politicians who care more about their own social media than getting actual results for families," South Carolina state Sen. JD Chaplin, and a spokesperson for Fry's campaign, told Fox News Digital. 

Norman was less venomous in his take on the situation, and noted on X that he respected Fry and had enjoyed his time serving with him in the House. Still, his candidacy, he argued, "could leave his seat vacant until May 18th."

"This would leave his constituents unrepresented, jeopardize President Trump's agenda, and put the House at risk of being led by a Speaker Hakeem Jeffries," Norman said. "We can't let that happen!!"

Senate Majority Leader Sen. John Thune, speaking to reporters on Monday afternoon, referred to Darlene Graham and said, "She obviously carries on Lindsey's legacy. But I think she's a very accomplished person in her own right."

Asked if he would be endorsing her, the longtime lawmaker from South Dakota and top Republican in the Senate said, "I'm not endorsing anyone at the moment."

Fox News' Tyler Olson contributed to this report

Original article

title: Opinion - Trump’s growing gang of untouchables
description: Trump has subverted equal justice to create two classes of citizens: one privileged, the other persecuted.
fullText:
One of a president's principal responsibilities under the U.S. Constitution is to ensure that the nation's laws are "faithfully executed." The Constitution also requires that laws be applied equally to all citizens. And in a tradition that goes back to the Magna Carta, our democracy operates on the assumption that no one — not even the president — is above the law.

That changed in July 2024, when the U.S. Supreme Court ruled that presidents cannot be prosecuted for breaking the law while performing their "core" duties. In addition, a Justice Department rule shields presidents from prosecution while they are in office.

Presidents can still be impeached and removed by Congress. The vice president can also orchestrate a president's removal if a majority of the Cabinet declares him unable to fulfill his duties. That process, authorized by Section 4 of the 25th Amendment, has never been used.

These sanctions have rarely been necessary, because most presidents have accepted moral and ethical accountability, even when their conduct is not strictly prohibited by law. President Trump is different. He is loath to accept any limits on his behavior, and the current Congress has refused to impose any.

Trump is not intimidated by his unprecedented pair of impeachments, nor by the 88 criminal charges filed against him between his terms in office, nor by his conviction on 34 criminal charges. He has carried his contempt for the law into the nation's highest office.

In his second term, Trump is doing more than ignoring legal and ethical constraints on his behavior. He is helping his allies escape legal accountability, too. He has subverted equal justice to create two classes of citizens: one privileged, the other persecuted.

The privileged group includes around 1,500 supporters who participated in the Capitol riot on Jan. 6, 2021. As president, Trump not only released them from jail and erased most of their criminal records, but he also wants to pay them millions of taxpayer dollars in restitution for the inconvenience of facing justice for trying to overturn a lawful election.

Trump has also granted pardons to people who have given him political donations and financial favors, as well as to colleagues with whom he has direct financial or political ties. This pay-to-play scheme is wildly unethical, but the Constitution does not limit the president's pardon power. At last count, Trump's pardons had not only deprived victims of justice; they also wiped out nearly $2 billion in court-ordered fines, forfeitures and restitution for victims and taxpayers.

This privileged group apparently includes the 1,835 loyalists Trump has appointed to federal jobs as well as his 22 Cabinet-level officers. He has promised to issue mass pardons to "everyone who has come within 200 feet of the Oval" before he leaves office.

Thus protected, members of his Cabinet have openly lied to Congress and ignored laws. For example, acting Attorney General Todd Blanche refuses to comply with the bipartisan law that requires him to release all files related to sex offender Jeffrey Epstein. The withheld files may contain additional references to Trump.

They also contain the allegations against the "Epstein class" — that is, other rich and prominent people who live lives of leisure, lack moral scruples and could have been involved in Epstein's crimes. The Justice Department has prosecuted none of them, except for Epstein's colleague, Ghislaine Maxwell.

The untouchables also appear to include tens of thousands of federal immigration and border officers, including some who should be charged with civil rights violations and even killings. They are allowed to wear masks, the kit of cowards and criminals, so that they can't be identified. It has months since ICE agents killed U.S. citizens Alex Pretti and Renee Good during protests in Minnesota. Both cases remain unresolved.

Trump has not ruled out pardoning himself for acts outside the Supreme Court's protection. He claims he has the "absolute right," although no previous president has exercised it.

What about the second group of thos whom Trump has turned the Justice Department against? The department is investigating, indicting or prosecuting Trump's perceived enemies using unsubstantiated allegations and the thinnest of pretexts. They include former FBI Director James Comey, indicted for taking and sharing a photo of seashells.

This group includes legal and illegal immigrants in the U.S., among them at least 3,800 children, arrested and detained, deported or imprisoned. Under the law, they have the same rights as citizens, yet many have been denied due process. This group also includes untold numbers of Americans the Trump administration brands as domestic terrorists and insurgents, simply because they lean left. Yet, the administration mostly ignores far-right groups whose history shows they are far more likely to be violent.

It is no longer accurate to say that Trump aspires to be an autocrat, king or dictator. With his self-serving sabotage of the rule of law, he is already there.

William S. Becker is a former U.S. Department of Energy central regional director and special assistant to the assistant secretary for energy efficiency and renewable energy. He is executive director of the Presidential Climate Action Project.

title: Despite Trump's unpopularity, new poll shows tight race for Congress
description: A new Washington Post poll shows a tight race for control of Congress as the November midterms near, and Donald Trump faces all-time low disapproval ratings.
fullText:
As President Donald Trump faces near-record-low approval ratings, a new Washington Post-Ipsos poll, conducted July 8-13, found that Democrats have a slim advantage over Republicans ahead of November's midterm elections.

Still, Democratic supporters are more motivated to vote. The WaPo-Ipsos poll shows Democrats leading Republicans by 3 percentage points, 48% to 45%, two years after Kamala Harris' 2024 loss to Trump.

The poll also found that Democrats' edge grows to 8 points among those "absolutely certain to vote" as 53% of highly motivated voters support Democrats while 45% support Republicans. Trump has faced low approval numbers amid issues with the economy and the Iran war.

The Post-Ipsos survey contrasted Democrats' current polling outcomes with 2018. Democrats had a 10-point lead in support for Congress ahead of his first presidential midterm election in which Democrats gained 40 U.S. House seats. Republicans maintained their majority in the Senate.

Trump continues to face waning approval numbers. His overall approval rating stands at 37 percent in the WaPo-Ipsos poll. A new Economist/YouGov poll, conducted July 10-13, found that the same number of Americans approve of his job performance.

What Trump family members will attend the FIFA World Cup Final?

House Speaker Mike Johnson currently has a two-vote margin and cannot afford defections as Republicans hope to enact Trump's agenda. Republicans currently have a 53-47 seat majority in the Senate. Partisan gerrymandering has thrust further uncertainty into Democrats' hopes to regain control of both chambers.

The poll found that Democrats are 10% more likely to certainly vote this year than Republicans, which eclipses their 2-point edge in 2018.

Contributing: Fernando Cervantes Jr., Zachary Schermele

This article originally appeared on USA TODAY: Despite Trump's unpopularity, new poll shows tight race for Congress

Spain Humiliates Trump After He Refuses to Get Out of Victory Photos
description: Donald Trump presented Spain’s World Cup team with the trophy ... and then refused to leave.
fullText:
Spain’s World Cup-winning team has made it abundantly clear that they did not want Donald Trump in their victory photos.

The official account for Spain’s national football league shared an altered image of the country’s celebration in New Jersey Sunday, snipping the U.S. president out of photos after he refused to leave the dais. The FIFA World Cup account also cropped Trump out of the shot, leaving only the president’s shoulder in view.

Trump handed the World Cup trophy to Spain’s team alongside FIFA President Gianni Infantino, after they beat Argentina 1-0. But the U.S. president then refused to leave the picture, apparently trying to run out time before the traditional trophy lift by chatting with the players as he jockeyed for a prime position in front of the group.

Spain’s team, however, was not having it. Video footage of the historic celebration captured several players politely pushing Trump away as they prepared for their official photograph.

Spain’s players refused to celebrate with Trump after the U.S. president attempted to insert himself into the middle of their victory photo. He was politely pushed aside. pic.twitter.com/x82yxxCZgw

— Anonymous (@YourAnonCentral) July 20, 2026

Yet Trump stayed anyway, lingering beside the team even after repeated attempts by Infantino to pull him off the stage. He finally exited only after the team lifted its trophy and blew the confetti cannons in a glaring attempt to ensure he’d be included in the photograph.

His insistence on remaining in frame followed weeks of scathing remarks by Trump against Spain and its prime minister, Pedro Sánchez, over NATO defense spending. Trump has referred to Spain as “hopeless,” derided its people as “bad,” and also threatened to “cut off all trade” with the longtime U.S. ally as he ramped up his NATO disputes. In the months prior to that, he also raged against Spain after they refused to let the U.S. military use Spanish bases to bomb Iran.

None of that hostility was on display after the game, however—at least publicly. Back at Joint Base Andrews, Trump said he had spoken to Spain and “congratulated them on having a great team.” He also noted that he had “no tension” with the country’s leadership.

“I have no tension with anybody,” Trump remarked cavalierly.

title: Trump calls for review related to scientific manual used by judges
description: U.S.
fullText:
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA, July 19 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump called for a review related to the ‌Federal Judicial Center's scientific reference manual in cases involving climate ‌change, writing in a Truth Social post on Sunday that the work contains "discredited" ​information that led to "huge losses" for the country.

"Our Nation's Federal Judges deserve Facts and Science, not Political Fraud and False Science on Climate," Trump posted. "With this TRUTH, I hereby order Federal Suspension and Debarment ‌Officials to review this ⁠conduct."

It was not immediately clear what "conduct" Trump wants investigated. The White House did not immediately reply to a ⁠request for comment.

Earlier this year, the U.S. federal judiciary withdrew a chapter from the newest edition of its reference manual on scientific ​evidence that ​addressed climate change after Republican state ​attorneys general argued it ‌was biased against fossil fuel companies.

The Federal Judicial Center, the judiciary's research arm, incorporated a chapter addressing climate change as part of a long-planned update of the manual, the fourth edition of which was released in December. Judges rely on the manual when grappling ‌with cases involving scientific testimony.

The section at ​issue in the fourth edition of the FJC's ​Reference Manual for Scientific Evidence ​was authored by Jessica Wentz and Radley Horton ‌of Columbia Law School and was ​drafted to "help judges ​evaluate the admissibility and weight of expert testimony and documentary evidence involving climate science."

The Federal Judicial Center jointly produced the ​manual with the ‌National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine.

title: Donald Trump wants World Cup back in U.S. 'immediately'
description: Donald Trump wants the World Cup in the U.S. again while still in office, but he will have to wait awhile for a return.
fullText:
The United States just finished hosting the 2026 World Cup for the first time in more than 30 years, and even though it will be a while before it does it again, President Donald Trump will be requesting to host it once more "immediately."

Trump made the comments to Fox Sports' Jenny Taft ahead of the World Cup final between Spain and Argentina on Sunday, July 19, lauding the success of the tournament that saw millions of people flock to the U.S., Canada and Mexico, and take part in a widely successful event.

He admitted to FIFA president Gianni Infantino that he was skeptical of how well the World Cup would do in the U.S., but the great turnout and reception resulted in him wanting it once more.

"I had a great idea for Gianni. I said, ‘You have to do two countries.’ So announce us again next time, and announce another country after that, that’ll take out some of the anger and the shock," Trump said. "But based on the numbers, we will be requesting it again immediately. But I said to take out the shock, you could announce us and maybe then for the next one another, and even another. But we have to do this again, and we have to do it while I’m around. You hear that, Gianni?”

If Trump was referring to the World Cup being in the U.S. again while he is in office, it won't happen since his term ends in 2028. The 2030 World Cup will be hosted by Morocco, Spain and Portugal, while the 2034 edition will be in Saudi Arabia.

The next available World Cup is in 2038, which the U.S. could very likely host since Oceania and North America will be eligible to host under the current cycle rules. However, that's in 12 years.

Trump has made his presence within FIFA known since 2018, when the U.S. was awarded the hosting duties for the 2026 tournament. He has become a close friend with Infantino, who presented Trump with the FIFA Peace Prize in December in a controversial move. He also inserted himself into the red card conversation surrounding U.S. star Folarin Balogun, when he pushed Infantino to reverse the decision to suspend him for one match, which ended up happening in another decision surrounded by controversy.

The president was inside MetLife Stadium for the final between Spain and Argentina, and was on the field to present the medals and trophy for Spain's title, which included him awkwardly still on stage as La Roja celebrated holding the trophy.

This article originally appeared on USA TODAY: Donald Trump wants World Cup back in U.S. 'immediately'

title: From Super Bowl to World Cup: The major sporting events Trump has attended in his 2nd term
description: President Donald Trump has prioritized attending huge sporting events during his second term.
fullText:
President Donald Trump has prioritized attending huge sporting events during his second term.

On Sunday, Trump will become the first U.S. president to attend a men’s World Cup final when Argentina and Spain face off in one of the year’s most anticipated sporting events. His appearance marks just the latest sporting event he will attend since returning to office in January 2025.

Here’s the list of other major sporting events he has attended since returning to office.

Trump became the first sitting U.S. president to attend a Super Bowl during on Feb. 9, 2025 in New Orleans. The game came about a month after a New Year’s Day terrorist attack in the city killed 14 people and injured dozens more. Trump met with families of the victims before the game.

Trump left the game about 10 minutes into the second half—which left pundits speculating that he left early because his desired team, the Kansas City Chiefs, was losing.

Trump, who is close with UFC President Dana White, has attended multiple fights during his second term in office. He was met with cheers when he attended a fight at the Kaseya Center in Miami alongside Secretary of State Marco Rubio in April 2025. He later attended a UFC match with Rubio at the Prudential Center in Newark, New Jersey in June 2025.

Trump reaffirmed his love for the UFC after he recently hosted a fight on the South Lawn of the White House on June 14 to celebrate America’s 250th anniversary.

Trump attended the 2025 Daytona 500 just weeks after going to Super Bowl LIX. He had also attended the popular race in 2020 during his first term in office. His appearance was marked by his motorcade taking a lap around the Daytona International Speedway.

Trump’s appearance at the World Cup final on Sunday is not the first time he attended a FIFA event. Last year, Trump was mocked after he awkwardly joined in on Chelsea FC’s FIFA Club World Cup celebration.

Chelsea winger Cole Palmer later said he did not realize Trump would be on the stage as his team lifted up their trophy.

“I knew he was going to be there, but I didn’t know he was going [to] be on the stand where we lift the trophy. So, I was a bit confused,” said Palmer, via USA TODAY.

Trump faced widespread criticism after his attendance at the U.S. Open men’s tennis final snarled security lines, which delayed fans getting into the USTA Billie Jean King National Tennis Center in September 2025.

The 2025 match between world No. 1 Jannik Sinner and No. 2 Carlos Alcaraz started about 30 minutes late as a result of enhanced security measures. Trump also faced backlash after it was reported that US Open officials sent an email to broadcasters asking them to censor any negative reactions to his presence.

Trump made waves after fans blamed the president for “cursing” the New York Knicks with his attendance at Game 3 of the NBA Finals at Madison Square Garden.

The NYPD and the Secret Service cancelled the popular watch party outside the world’s most famous arena, leaving die-hard fans outraged. Fans were also prohibited from bringing bags inside the arena, and were told to expect enhanced security measures before entering the game.

Trump was also met with boos as he was shown on screen before tip-off.

The Knicks ended up breaking their 13-game winning streak in Game 3, making it their only loss in the best-of-seven matchup with the San Antonio Spurs.

title: Is Trump the first president to attend a World Cup final?
description: President Donald Trump will be attending the final men’s World Cup game in East Rutherford, New Jersey on Sunday afternoon.
fullText:
President Donald Trump will be attending the final men’s World Cup game in East Rutherford, New Jersey on Sunday afternoon.

Argentina and Spain are set to face off for the World Cup final at 3 p.m. ET in East Rutherford, New Jersey after a month of thrilling games that have gripped sports fans across the globe. The White House confirmed last week that Trump will be attending Sunday’s highly anticipated game as the U.S. caps off hosting one of the largest sporting events in the world.

“His attendance will cap what has been the most watched, most secure, and most successful World Cup in American history,” White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt told reporters. “This is a fitting conclusion to a tournament that showcased America’s ability to host the world on the grandest stage.”

FIFA president Gianni Infantino, a close ally of the president, also confirmed last month on “Fox & Friends” that he and Trump will jointly present the trophy to the winners of the World Cup.

“We will be together with the president enjoying the final and handing the trophy to the winner, of course, together,” Infantino said.

Trump faced criticism earlier this month after it was reported that he called Infantino asking FIFA to overturn the suspension of U.S. men’s national team striker Folarin Balogun. Balogun was ultimately allowed to play, but the U.S. men’s national team still lost to Belgium.

The appearance will mark the first time a U.S. president will attend the men’s World Cup final. The U.S. has hosted the tournament only once before, under then-President Bill Clinton.

When the U.S. last hosted the men’s World Cup in 1994, Clinton delivered remarks at the opening ceremony but did not attend the final match between Brazil and Italy. Instead, then-Vice President Al Gore attended the final game in Pasadena, California and handed the trophy to Brazil’s team captain.

But Clinton did attend the women’s final game in Pasadena when the U.S. women’s soccer team clinched their second World Cup title in 1999.

title: Spain win World Cup after Messi's Argentina fall flat
description: Spain won their second World Cup on Sunday, collecting the trophy from US President Donald Trump after Ferran Torres' extra-time strike broke the stubborn resistance of Lionel Messi's Argentina.Argentina coach Lionel Scaloni admitted Spain had deserved to win.
fullText:
Spain won their second World Cup on Sunday, collecting the trophy from US President Donald Trump after Ferran Torres' extra-time strike broke the stubborn resistance of Lionel Messi's Argentina.

More than 80,000 people crammed into the MetLife Stadium in East Rutherford, New Jersey, to witness a tense, tight final punctuated by cynical Argentine fouls that ended 1-0.

The defending champions struggled to compete with dominant Spain and were left with a mountain to climb when Enzo Fernandez was sent off in the 93rd minute for a second yellow card after a reckless challenge on defender Pau Cubarsi.

Argentina barely had an attempt on goal in the entire match but held on grimly, with goalkeeper Emiliano Martinez making a series of saves.

The 2010 winners, who had peppered their opponents' goal, finally broke the deadlock early in the second half of extra-time.

The goal came from a brilliant knock-back by Nico Williams for fellow substitute Torres, who smashed the ball high into the net in the 106th minute.

At the end, the Spain players cavorted on the pitch while Messi was in tears as he trudged up to collect his runners-up medal.

The ceremony was presided over by Trump, who was loudly booed as he walked onto the pitch at the end of the game with Gianni Infantino, the chief of world football's governing body FIFA.

Trump had to be asked by Infantino to move to the side as he lingered on stage while the Spanish team held the trophy aloft.

The 80-year-old even did a quick version of his trademark "Trump dance".

An emotional Spain coach Luis de la Fuente said his players were a "source of pride" for the nation.

Spain captain Rodri, who won the player of the tournament award, said it was hard to put his feelings into words, 14 months after he returned from a serious knee injury.

"I think now we're like in a cloud," he said. "It's been a very tough time. I just want the new generations to see my example as an opportunity -- when you go down you can rise again."

Messi, 39, may have played his last World Cup game and he can take enormous credit for dragging his ageing team to the final after they were pushed to the limit in knockout games against Egypt and England.

But on Sunday, he was largely shut out of the game as Spain imposed their smooth passing game and refused to shirk physical challenges.

The South Americans had been aiming to become the first team since Brazil in 1962 to win consecutive World Cups. But Spain were faster, slicker and younger.

Argentina coach Lionel Scaloni admitted Spain had deserved to win.

"They were the better side -- that's the truth -- but I'll cherish the memory of (this team), of what they achieved, and of the value of making it this far," said Scaloni, who later broke down in tears at his post-game press conference.

"We have to place immense value on this because it takes so much effort," he added.

Musical headliners Madonna, Shakira, Justin Bieber, BTS and Coldplay were joined by Brazilian soccer icons Ronaldinho and Ronaldo and "Ted Lasso" actors in the first-ever World Cup final half-time show.

The star-packed, Super Bowl-style extravaganza also featured a tribute to late football icon Pele, and a rendition of "Seven Nation Army" led by Gustavo Dudamel and featuring musicians from the US, Venezuela and Iran.

On Saturday, England beat France 6-4 in a high-scoring thriller to seal third place -- their best finish since they won the tournament in 1966.

Kylian Mbappe became the competition's all-time leading scorer with 22 goals.

The France forward also won the Golden Boot with 10 goals, two ahead of Messi, finishing as top scorer for the second World Cup in a row.

The build-up to the World Cup in Canada, Mexico and the United States was dominated by fears over sky-high prices, geopolitical tensions and concerns that brutal summer heat would make matches unplayable.

But the biggest, most complex edition of football's showpiece ever staged -- expanded from 32 teams to 48 -- will be remembered for thrilling football and a feel-good factor.

Trump has hailed the tournament as "the most successful sporting event, maybe in the history of the world".

title: Fetterman on Trump election speech: ‘Do something positive, and you are talking about 2020’
description: Sen. John Fetterman (D-Pa.) on Sunday said President Trump could “do something positive” instead of talking about the 2020 presidential election, clapping at the president’s primetime speech on Thursd...
fullText:
Sen. John Fetterman (D-Pa.) on Sunday said President Trump could "do something positive" instead of talking about the 2020 presidential election, clapping at the president's primetime speech on Thursday about election security.

"I don't know why the president is dragging up all this stuff," Fetterman told CNN's Jake Tapper on "State of the Union." "You're the president. Do something positive, and you are talking about 2020. That's been settled over six years ago. It's deeply unhelpful and that distracts important things, especially like the Iranian war right now."

On Thursday, Trump said recently declassified documents suggest China interfered in the 2020 presidential election by acquiring more than 200 million U.S. voter files. A 2021 report from the intelligence community about foreign threats to the 2020 elections concluded with "high confidence" that China did not interfere in the election but did take steps to try to undermine Trump's reelection.

Fetterman, who was Pennsylvania's lieutenant governor at the time, said the 2020 presidential election was "absolutely secure, fair and accurate."

"That was the voice of Pennsylvania, and they picked Joe Biden in 2020, that's a fact," Fetterman said. "… Now, until I see evidence that over 25 million migrants that … voted or registered to vote, then yeah, that would make me reconsider some of these things. But there's no evidence, and I don't expect that we'll see any of that."

The Pennsylvania Democrat questioned "why the president is dragging up all of this stuff."

Trump used his primetime speech, amid the backdrop of his ongoing war with Iran, to accuse the intelligence community of failing to sound the alarm as China penetrated U.S. voter rolls. Another confusing claim was that the intelligence community "worked to actively suppress and downplay information about the extent of China's sinister election meddling, covering it up from both the president and the American people."

Trump used the speech to suggest that there should be changes to U.S. elections, including the passage of his legislative priority requiring proof of citizenship to register to vote and photo ID to cast a ballot.

"Bogus" was how Sen. Mark Warner (Va.), the top Democrat on the Senate Intelligence Committee, described Trump's claims.

"The fact is our intelligence agencies unanimously agreed that China did not even try to change a single vote in the 2020 election," Warner wrote on social platform X. "A single concurring opinion suggested China may have tried to sway voters' opinions… but that's been public knowledge since 2021."

Some Republican lawmakers criticized the speech before and after it aired. Rep. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.) said on Friday after the speech that the problem was not that the elections were "stolen," adding that Republicans "won the damn elections."

"White House, Senate, House, even the Supreme Court leans our way," Massie wrote on X. "The fraud is that our party got elected and broke our campaign promises to voters."

title: Trump, Bieber and Shakira - a World Cup final like never before
description: The 2026 World Cup final may not linger long in the memory for what happened on the pitch but it will for all that surrounded it, with an appearance by President Trump, the longest half-time in football and songs by a host of stars.
fullText:
The 2026 World Cup final might not be remembered as a superb football match but it will at least go down in history for all that surrounded it.

There was the longest half-time in history, featuring performances from artists including Madonna and Justin Bieber, a pre-kick-off speech from Hollywood A-lister Tom Cruise, an appearance by US president Donald Trump and fireworks. Lots of fireworks - all in broad daylight.

Spain won the trophy for the second time, beating Argentina through a Ferran Torres goal in extra time but there was little else to talk about in the game itself. Plenty happened around the game though, with these some of the most memorable moments.

Spain's 'victory for football' over 'embarrassing' Argentina

Lamine Yamal completes football at 19 as Argentina betray Messi

Shakira, Madonna, Justin Bieber and BTS perform at colourful World Cup half-time show

This World Cup was co-hosted by the USA, Mexico and Canada but it has been the United States which has garnered much of the attention, particularly around comments or actions by Trump.

He previously stated that this has been a World Cup like no other - and he's certainly not wrong about that, not least with the controversy of US striker Folarin Balogun avoiding a ban despite being sent off .

That came after Trump admitted he had spoken to Fifa president Gianni Infantino, asking him to review the sending off. The lifting of the suspension meant Balogun was able to play for the USA in the last-16 loss to Belgium.

Perhaps just as surprising was Trump's lack of attendance at games throughout the tournament. He had not been to a single match prior to the final, meaning all eyes were on his appearance at New York New Jersey Stadium on Sunday.

His arrival was visible to those in the stadium as he was in one of three helicopters that flew overhead before doing a circuit of it and then landing.

He appeared to take his seat - alongside Infantino - about 20 minutes before kick-off, just in time to watch Robbie Williams and Nicole Scherzinger perform in the closing ceremony.

He was later seen in the first half being shown the trophy.

As well as Trump's appearance, the other big talking point in the lead up to the final was the Super Bowl-style half-time show.

While the mid-game break is usually for players to recover from first-half exertion, coaches to give a team-talk and fans to buy food and drinks, this time it was extended to allow a mini-concert to take place.

There were some big names featured - Madonna, BTS, Coldplay and Shakira, whose song 'Dai Dai' is the official anthem of the World Cup - all starred, while there was also an appearance by Justin Bieber.

After getting a pep talk from Ted Lasso and his assistant coach Beard, Bieber changed the pace of the otherwise energetic performances with an acoustic guitar version of 'Everything Hallelujah'.

The half-time interval lasted 27 minutes, but did it work? The reaction among fans was a bit mixed - but inside the Match of the Day studio, the assessment by former England striker Wayne Rooney was pretty blunt.

"I thought it was crap," he said. "I thought the performances were bad. The timings were bad. Just let us play football."

Meanwhile, the closing ceremony before the match was similar in style to many other such ceremonies with loud music, dancing on the pitch though with the addition too of a somewhat dramatic speech by actor Cruise.

For neutrals, one of the most-anticipated post-match moments centred around how visible a part Trump would play in the trophy presentation.

At the end of last year's Club World Cup final, Trump got caught up with the Chelsea celebrations after presenting the trophy to captain Reece James.

On that occasion, having handed the main prize over, he remained on the stage for a few seconds alongside James and goalkeeper Robert Sanchez before eventually departing to the rear.

"They told me that he was going to present the trophy and then exit the stage... but he wanted to stay," said James, who was part of England's squad for this year's World Cup.

At the end of Sunday's World Cup final, Trump - who had joined Infantino in handing out the medals to both the winning and losing players - did linger again for the trophy presentation, although not for quite as long as he did for the Club World Cup.

There had also been boos when Trump and Infantino emerged for the trophy ceremony while at least one player also appeared to choose not to shake Trump's hand, with Argentina defender Cristian Romero walking straight past him after receiving his medal from Infantino.

This was certainly a glamorous World Cup final and there was no shortage of fireworks - they went off before kick-off, during the half-time show and after Spain's trophy lift.

But the hours beforehand will have felt anything but glamorous as heightened security meant those getting into the ground faced long queues.

With Trump attending in person, the United States secret service took over security for the game.

Gates for spectators opened at 11:00 local time - four hours before kick-off but stadium staff, performers and media had been arriving since much earlier.

Confusion over access gates had led to some members of the media being told to go to one entrance, only to be told they had to go back and start again at the queue they had been in previously.

Bag checks were also more thorough than in previous games, contributing to the length of time people waited to get in.

Despite that, congestion was eventually eased and the game kicked off without any delays.

When the World Cup finishes, the football continues on BBC Sport

title: Donald Trump goes off-script on 'bagpipes' tangent in World Cup Final interview
description: President Donald Trump was interviewed ahead of the FIFA World Cup Final between Argentina and Spain, but the 80-year-old went off-script and began rambling about Scotland and bagpipes during the conversation
fullText:
President Donald Trump went off on a tangent during a pre-match interview ahead of the FIFA World Cup Final.

On July 19, Argentina faces Spain in the Final of the World Cup. The two nations battle for the world championship title at MetLife Stadium, branded as New York/New Jersey Stadium by the tournament, in East Rutherford, New Jersey.

Prior to the match on Fox Sports Live, President Donald Trump, 80, was questioned about how the United States Men's National Team had captured the world's attention, via The Mirror US.

Donald Trump sparks outrage with Harry Kane World Cup comments

Kylian Mbappe drops World Cup truth bomb after absurd third-place loss to England

Instead, Trump veered off-topic and began talking about Scotland — this following his boasts about pressuring FIFA chief Gianni Infantino over USMNT striker Folarin Balogun's red card, which triggered widespread controversy.

TV personality Jenny Taft, 38, posed the question: "It has been so special to see fans all over the world fall in love with our country. How proud does that make you?"

Trump responded: "Well, I watched, and it started with Scotland, and all of a sudden they're doing things that nobody has ever seen with the bagpipes. And it was beautiful.

"And it just went to every country. And we're the hottest country in the world right now," he concluded.

Trump's previous World Cup comments also drew significant backlash. England head coach Thomas Tuchel delivered a sharp rebuke after the President took aim at the team following their World Cup semifinal defeat to Argentina, during which Trump specifically criticized the tactical deployment of captain Harry Kane.

To listen to the latest episode of 'All Out Soccer' - CLICK HERE

"Harry, who's been fantastic, I think they perhaps made a mistake when they made him a defensive player. What do I know about soccer?" Trump initially said.

"They took the lead, and they took their best player and then put him on defense. What do I know about coaching? But that was a little unusual," he closed.

Tuchel was questioned about Trump's remarks ahead of England's third-place match against France on July 18. "Do you use Donald Trump as your witness for the case?" the ex-Chelsea boss responded to the reporter.

England then produced their finest performance of the tournament. England defeated France 6-4 in a thriller.

Following the victory over the French, Tuchel dismissed Trump's criticism and declared, "This game will help us for sure – even if you can never fully celebrate a bronze medal.

"It's the first World Cup medal for 60 years, first at a World Cup on foreign soil, and I hope the players can be proud of that at some point.

"We set ourselves the highest dream to chase, and we were very, very ambitious with our dream to make the final in the World Cup. So it's very, very painful if you miss out; the pain will stay for a while," he finished.

title: Trump arrives at World Cup final for Argentina vs. Spain matchup
description: Trump will watch final as he demands U.S. host World Cup again ‘immediately’
fullText:
Donald Trump arrived at the final of the World Cup on Sunday afternoon, where the U.S. president will take in his first and only match of the championship as Argentina and Spain battle it out for the trophy.

Pool reports indicated that Trump departed Bedminster, New Jersey via Marine One just before 1:00 p.m. eastern time, and reporters were quickly afterwards ushered onto the field for the pre-game events. His helicopter completed a brief flyover, which went largely unnoticed by the crowd, according to reporters in the stadium. Marine One then touched down outside the arena.

The president hosted a press conference on Friday as he hosted a reception with FIFA’s president, Gianni Infantino, and said that he’d bring up the idea of hosting the championship again to Infantino. Hosting rights for the next two World Cups have already been decided.

This is a breaking news report. More to follow...

title: Donald Trump predicts the winner of Spain vs Argentina FIFA World Cup final
description: Spain vs Argentina will be the 23rd edition of the FIFA World Cup final at the MetLife Stadium on Sunday evening as the best tournament on earth comes to an end for another four years. Of the 48 final teams, just two remain as the champions of Europe, Spain, face defending world champions Argentina.
fullText:
Spain vs Argentina will be the 23rd edition of the FIFA World Cup final at the MetLife Stadium on Sunday evening as the best tournament on earth comes to an end for another four years.

Of the 48 final teams, just two remain as the champions of Europe, Spain, face defending world champions Argentina.

Soccer fans across the globe are divided on who will be crowned champions ahead of kick-off and naturally, US President Donald Trump was asked for his prediction.

Jenny Taft of Fox Sports caught up with Mr Trump hours before kick-off as the president prepared to take his seat at the biggest sporting occasion on the planet.

He was asked: “I’ve got to ask, Argentina-Spain, give us your prediction.”

He said: “Well, I knew you were going to ask, so I thought about it very hard. I hate to get involved even though politically it doesn’t matter too much.

“The head of Argentina is a friend of mine who has done a terrific job, but I would say it’s hard to bet against Messi. I watched that pass (vs England) I think you’d call it a pass. It was exactly perfect.

“It takes some talent to hit the ball off the head coming at that speed, but that was perfect if you had a strike zone, that would be perfect. Just before that, the way he got away from the defender, he was defended very well; all of a sudden, he’s standing by himself, so it’s hard to bet against him.

“I won’t pick sides but I think it’s very hard to bet against Messi; he’s great.”

‘They’d call us animals’ – World Cup final security slammed amid two-hour queues and ‘total chaos’

title: Trump presence means tight security for World Cup final
description: Supporters faced long queues to get into New York New Jersey Stadium amid heightened security for the 2026 World Cup final.
fullText:
Supporters faced long queues to get into New York New Jersey Stadium amid heightened security for the 2026 World Cup final.

Spain face Argentina at the 82,500-seater venue, with kick-off at 15:00 local time (20:00 BST).

There is increased security partly because United States president Donald Trump is attending the final - the only World Cup match he is watching in person.

As a result, the United States Secret Service took over security for the game - leading to long waits for supporters to get into the ground.

BBC Sport understands people were having to queue for about two hours.

But after the long queues, it is understood most fans had no issues scanning tickets and accessing the stadium.

There were two levels of security at the stadium, run by both the Secret Service and Transportation Security Administration (TSA).

The latter is usually responsible for security at airports in the United States, with fans and media having to go through airport security-style scanners.

One member of the media said they, having reached an entrance, were told to go to a different queue as there were no security dogs available to search bags at that gate.

BBC Sport has been told by World Cup volunteers at the ground that the number of people arriving early before the game was much higher than the previous seven matches at the stadium.

Gates opened for supporters at 11:00 local time - four hours before kick-off - with more gates being opened sporadically to ease congestion.

It is not thought there will be any delay to kick-off or any other disruption to the match.

title: Stephen Miller says Trump administration is pursuing policy to debank illegal immigrants
description: Stephen Miller says the Trump administration is debanking illegal immigrants to shut down their financial access and drive self-deportation.
fullText:
White House deputy chief of staff Stephen Miller said Friday that the Trump administration is seeking to debank illegal immigrants as an incentive for them to self-deport from the United States.

Miller gave an update on the White House's latest tactic to deter illegal immigration during an interview Friday on "The Clay Travis & Buck Sexton show."

"President Trump signed an executive order a few weeks ago saying that we are not going to allow illegal aliens to use banking services in this country," Miller said.

"Illegal aliens have credit cards, they have bank accounts and they're paid with direct deposit. So, illegal aliens fully participate in the commercial systems, the financial systems of America. Shutting that down is a massive engine for deportation."

Obama-appointed Judge Torpedoes TRUMP'S Bid To Protect Us Jobs By Reforming Controversial Immigration Policy

Miller was likely referring to an executive order Trump signed on May 19 that directed banks and federal regulators to increase scrutiny of accounts and credit applications involving immigrants without legal status or work authorization.

The order did not explicitly instruct banks to deny credit cards or bank accounts to illegal immigrants, but compliance with it could make it more difficult for them to participate in the financial system.

Federal agencies have already begun acting on Trump's executive order.

Trump Administration Orders Green Card Applicants To Leave The Us, Apply From Their Home Countries

In early June, the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, led by Russell Vought, issued guidance stating that lenders may — and in some cases must — consider applicants' immigration status and work authorization when assessing their ability to repay loans.

Miller also referenced guidance from three federal agencies issued on July 13, "reminding supervised financial institutions to apply existing safe-and-sound credit risk management practices when lending to borrowers who are not legally authorized to work in the United States."

Both advisories serve to tell banks that illegal immigrants could be higher-risk customers for loans.

Fox News Digital reached out to the White House for comment.
